context: Ecclesiastes in chapter number 7
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 7:2's counterintuitive declaration that the house of mourning surpasses the house of feasting frames the sermon's engagement with Sarah's death as spiritually profitable rather than merely somber.

context: For the Bible says in Ecclesiastes 7 and verse number 2, it is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting.
point_summary: Confronting death in the house of mourning is spiritually profitable because its sobering reality compels the living to take mortality to heart in a way that feasting cannot.

context: Man goes to his long home, Ecclesiastes says.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Solomon says it is better to go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting, for this is the end of all men.
point_summary: Confronting death in "the house of mourning" strips away life's illusions and reminds the living that this present existence is temporary, pressing the unsaved toward their need for Christ.

context: You understand what Solomon said? There's a time to be born, there's a time to die. In fact, it's interesting and it's something that, you know, maybe sometimes we shy away from this truth, but we know that it's a biblical truth and we find that Solomon says this in Ecclesiastes 7 and verse number 2, it is better to go
point_summary: Death's universality — "the end of all men" — makes confronting mortality in the house of mourning more profitable than avoiding it in the house of feasting.

context: to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting, for that is the end of all men, and the living will lay it to his heart.
point_summary: Death's universality — "the end of all men" — is the biblical reality that makes the house of mourning more instructive than feasting, and the proper response for the living is to take it to heart.

context: let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter. Fear God and keep his commandments, for this is the whole duty of man.
point_summary: After surveying the vanity of all earthly pursuits, Solomon's final conclusion — fear God and keep his commandments — stands as the whole duty of man, echoing what Moses required of Israel before entering Canaan.

context: let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter. Fear God, keep His commandments. This is the whole duty of man.
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 12:13's summation — "fear God, keep His commandments" — is presented as the capstone of divine instruction, paralleling Micah 6:8's call to justice, mercy, and humility as God's clear, sufficient requirement for His people.

context: Man, according to Ecclesiastes 12, goes to his long home.
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 12's declaration that "man goes to his long home" grounds the urgency of redeeming time: life is finite and irretrievable, making every moment a stewardship opportunity before eternity.

context: Ecclesiastes chapter number four and verse number 12, and if one prevail against him, two shall withstand him. Notice, a threefold cord is not quickly broken.
point_summary: A threefold cord not quickly broken illustrates why Jesus, rather than simply reasserting His own testimony, marshals three distinct witnesses to establish His identity before His Jewish accusers.

reference_text: a time to be born and a time to die
context: The Bible tells us that there's a time to be born and a time to die.
point_summary: Life's boundaries—birth and death—are set by God, underscoring that physical existence is not ultimate; eternal life offered through Christ is what matters most.

context: The wise man says in Ecclesiastes that man goes to his long home.
point_summary: Ecclesiastes' phrase "his long home" is cited to affirm that every person faces eternity, and that believers can rejoice because heaven, not this world, is their true home.

context: Ecclesiastes says this, that it's better to go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 7:2 is invoked to justify introducing death into evangelistic conversation, since mortality is universal and sober reflection on it opens hearts to the gospel.

context: The wise man said in Ecclesiastes 12 in verse number five that man goes to his long home.
point_summary: The brevity of earthly life—man going to "his long home"—establishes eternity's certainty as a compelling motive for bold gospel proclamation.

reference_text: There's not a just man upon the face of the earth
context: There's not a just man upon the face of the earth.
point_summary: Sin's universality—grounding why Christ had to die for sins—is affirmed by appeal to the truth that there is not a just man upon the face of the earth.

context: he would go to his long home
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 12:5's phrase "go to his long home" is applied to Paul's departure from this temporal life into eternity, framing death as the believer's entrance into the presence of Christ.

context: the wise man says in Ecclesiastes 2
point_summary: Earthly labor and accumulated treasure cannot be carried beyond death, making present service to God—with time, talents, and treasure—the only opportunity available to the living.

context: therefore I hated life, because the work that is wrought under the sun is grievous unto me, for all is vanity and vexation of spirit.
point_summary: Solomon's lament that death ends all earthly labor (Eccl. 2:17–19) is used to press the urgency of present giving—of time, talent, and treasure—while one still lives.

context: Yea, I hated all my labor which I had taken under the sun. Why? Because I should leave it unto the man that shall be after me.
point_summary: Solomon's lament that he must leave his labor to another after death underscores the urgency of faithful stewardship—giving of time, talents, and treasure—while one still lives on this side of eternity.

context: And who knoweth whether he shall be a wise man or a fool? Yet shall he have rule over all my labor wherein I have labored, and wherein I have showed myself wise under the sun. This is also vanity.
point_summary: Solomon's lament that he cannot control what happens to his labor after death underscores the urgency of faithful stewardship—giving of time, talents, and treasure—while one still lives on this side of eternity.

context: Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with thy might. For there's no work, nor device, nor knowledge, nor wisdom in the grave, whither thou goest.
point_summary: Work done in this life carries eternal urgency, so whatever God calls believers to do must be pursued with full effort, since no opportunity for such labor remains beyond the grave.

context: Whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it with all thy might
point_summary: Wholehearted effort in every task—even menial service like cleaning bathrooms—honors God, because whatever one's hand finds to do is to be done with all one's might.

context: ...because sentence against an evil work is not executed speedily, therefore the heart of the sons of men is fully set in them to do evil.
point_summary: Delayed execution of justice, Ecclesiastes 8:11 warns, emboldens the wicked — grounding the sermon's argument that once guilt is established, capital punishment should be carried out speedily.

context: There's not a just man, a righteous man, that walketh upon the face of the earth,
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: it's better that we would go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting
context: we're told by Solomon that it's better that we would go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting
point_summary: Attending a funeral ("the house of mourning") is more profitable than feasting because death is the end of all men, prompting reflection on what one's own eulogy will reveal about a life lived for God.

context: Fear God and keep His commandments. And we find that as we come to the New Testament...
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 12:13's summation of human duty—fear God and keep His commandments—serves as the Old Testament foundation from which the sermon's New Testament theme of walking worthy and pleasing God is launched.

context: to everything there is a season and a time to every purpose under the heaven
point_summary: Every human life is bounded by a divinely appointed season—a time to be born and a time to die—and while we know our birth date, the moment of death remains unknown to us.

context: a time to be born and a time to die
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 3:2 establishes that while every person knows the date of their birth, the time of death remains unknown—fixed by God but hidden from us.

context: In chapter number seven of the book of Ecclesiastes, notice what the Bible says in verse number two. Here in Ecclesiastes 7, in verse number two, the Bible says it is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting.
point_summary: Attending a funeral is better than feasting because it confronts the living with the unavoidable reality that death is the end of all men, prompting sober reflection on one's own mortality.

context: man goeth to his long home
point_summary: Ecclesiastes 12:5's phrase "man goeth to his long home" is invoked to underscore that eternity is inescapable, intensifying the weight of the wrath that abides on those who reject the Son.

context: whatsoever thy hand findeth to do, do it heartily as unto the Lord
point_summary: Doing everything wholeheartedly "as unto the Lord" reframes earthly diligence as service directed toward God's eternal reward rather than temporal recognition.
